quizQuestion:
{___} llueva, iremos al partido.
(Even if it rains, we'll go to the match.)
[Hint: unknown/hypothetical — use subjunctive form after blank]
sentenceTranslation: Even if it rains, we'll go to the match.
explanation: aunque — concessive connector meaning 'even if' (with subjunctive) or 'even though' (with indicative). Aunque + subjunctive when the situation is unknown or hypothetical. Aunque llueva = even if it rains (we don't know if it will).

quizQuestion:
{___} estaba cansada, siguió trabajando.
(Even though she was tired, she kept working.)
[Hint: known fact — indicative follows]
sentenceTranslation: Even though she was tired, she kept working.
explanation: aunque — 'even though' with indicative when the situation is a known fact. Aunque estaba cansada (we know she was tired = indicative). Contrast: aunque esté cansada (even if she is — hypothetical = subjunctive).

quizQuestion:
{___} estudies, no aprenderás en un día.
(No matter how much you study, you won't learn in a day.)
sentenceTranslation: No matter how much you study, you won't learn in a day.
grammarPointLabel: concessive connector: por mucho que + subjunctive
explanation: Por mucho que — 'no matter how much' + subjunctive. Always followed by subjunctive. Contrast: por mucho que estudies (subj) = no matter how much you study. Equivalent: por más que estudies.

quizQuestion:
{___} estaba cansado, siguió trabajando.
(Despite being tired, he kept working.)
sentenceTranslation: Despite being tired, he kept working.
grammarPointLabel: concessive connector: a pesar de que + indicative (known concession)
explanation: A pesar de que — 'despite the fact that / despite + -ing'. Followed by indicative when the situation is factual. A pesar de (without que) is followed by a noun or infinitive: a pesar de la lluvia / a pesar de estar cansado.

quizQuestion:
{___} no tienes dinero, te presto yo.
(Since you don't have money, I'll lend you some.)
sentenceTranslation: Since you don't have money, I'll lend you some.
explanation: Puesto que — formal causal connector meaning 'since / given that'. Always followed by indicative. More formal than ya que or porque. Common in written and formal speech.

quizQuestion:
{___} estás aquí, ayúdame con esto.
(Since you're here, help me with this.)
sentenceTranslation: Since you're here, help me with this.
explanation: ya que — causal connector meaning 'since / seeing that'. Followed by indicative. Less formal than puesto que. Common in everyday speech when giving a reason.

quizQuestion:
{___} no hay más preguntas, concluimos la reunión.
(Given that there are no more questions, we'll conclude the meeting.)
sentenceTranslation: Given that there are no more questions, we'll conclude the meeting.
explanation: Dado que — 'given that / since'. Formal causal connector, always with indicative. Used in formal/professional contexts. Similar to puesto que but slightly more formal.

quizQuestion:
{___} la lluvia, cancelamos el partido.
(Due to the rain, we cancelled the match.)
sentenceTranslation: Due to the rain, we cancelled the match.
grammarPointLabel: causal connector: debido a (due to + noun)
explanation: Debido a — 'due to / because of'. Followed by a noun (not a clause): debido a la lluvia. With a clause: debido a que + indicative. It's a formal causal expression.

quizQuestion:
No estudié, {___} suspendí el examen.
(I didn't study, therefore I failed the exam.)
sentenceTranslation: I didn't study, therefore I failed the exam.
explanation: por lo tanto — 'therefore / thus / consequently'. Consecutive connector showing that the second clause results from the first. Can be placed at the start of the second clause with a comma before it.

quizQuestion:
Llegué tarde, {___} me perdí el principio.
(I arrived late, so I missed the beginning.)
sentenceTranslation: I arrived late, so I missed the beginning.
explanation: así que — 'so / therefore'. Informal consecutive connector. More common in spoken Spanish than por lo tanto. The second clause shows the direct result or consequence of the first.

quizQuestion:
Habló despacio, {___} todos le entendieron.
(He spoke slowly, so that everyone understood him.)
sentenceTranslation: He spoke slowly, so that everyone understood him.
grammarPointLabel: consecutive connector: de modo que (so that / with the result that)
explanation: de modo que — 'so that / with the result that'. With indicative: shows the actual result achieved. With subjunctive: shows intended purpose (like para que). De modo que + indicative emphasizes the result.

quizQuestion:
{___} llegues, llámame.
(As soon as you arrive, call me.)
[Hint: future temporal — subjunctive follows]
sentenceTranslation: As soon as you arrive, call me.
explanation: en cuanto — 'as soon as'. Followed by subjunctive when referring to a future event. En cuanto llegues (subj) = as soon as you arrive. With past events: en cuanto llegó (indicative) = as soon as he arrived.

quizQuestion:
{___} hayas terminado, puedes irte.
(As soon as you have finished, you can go.)
sentenceTranslation: As soon as you have finished, you can go.
grammarPointLabel: temporal connector: tan pronto como + present perfect subjunctive
explanation: Tan pronto como — 'as soon as'. Like en cuanto, followed by subjunctive for future events. Here hayas terminado = present perfect subjunctive (the finishing must happen before leaving). Interchangeable with en cuanto.

quizQuestion:
{___} hayas comido, descansa un poco.
(When you have eaten, rest a little.)
[Hint: future event — subjunctive required]
sentenceTranslation: When you have eaten, rest a little.
explanation: cuando — 'when'. With indicative for past/present facts: cuando llegó (when he arrived). With subjunctive for future events: cuando hayas comido (when you have eaten — future). This cuando + subj rule is a key B1/B2 point.

quizQuestion:
Vendré {___} llueva.
(I'll come unless it rains.)
grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: a menos que + subjunctive (unless)
explanation: a menos que — 'unless'. Always followed by subjunctive. A menos que llueva = unless it rains. The subjunctive is mandatory after a menos que regardless of tense.

quizQuestion:
Puedes ir {___} vuelvas pronto.
(You can go as long as you come back soon.)
sentenceTranslation: You can go as long as you come back soon.
grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: con tal de que + subjunctive (as long as)
explanation: con tal de que — 'as long as / provided that'. Always followed by subjunctive. Con tal de que vuelvas = as long as you come back. The condition is required for the main action to be permitted.

quizQuestion:
Lleva paraguas {___} llueva.
(Take an umbrella in case it rains.)
sentenceTranslation: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: en caso de que + subjunctive (in case)
explanation: en caso de que — 'in case'. Always followed by subjunctive. En caso de que llueva = in case it rains. Used for precautions against possible (but not expected) events.

quizQuestion:
{___} estudies, aprobarás.
(As long as you study, you'll pass.)
[Hint: conditional 'as long as' + subjunctive]
sentenceTranslation: As long as you study, you'll pass.
grammarPointLabel: conditional connector: siempre que + subjunctive (as long as)
explanation: siempre que — 'as long as / whenever' + subjunctive. Siempre que estudies = as long as you study. When used conditionally (as long as), it takes subjunctive. When factual (every time/whenever), it takes indicative.
